title: What is English devolution and what are the likely next steps?
gist: With Andy Burnham expected to become UK Prime Minister, English devolution moves up the agenda, but Oxford Economics expects continued gradualism: the biggest advance will be incremental revenue retention for mature Mayoral Authorities, which risks entrenching regional inequality because the fastest-growing regions are already the wealthiest.

themes.theme: Political catalyst and the nature of devolution

insights.claim: Andy Burnham, former Mayor of Greater Manchester, is expected to become UK Prime Minister within a week, pushing devolution to the top of the political agenda.
insights.speaker: Suki Masana
insights.so_what: A pro-devolution PM signals policy tailwinds for regional fiscal reform and city-region investment; watch Mayoral Authorities as beneficiaries of shifting power and budgets.
insights.quote: With Andy Burnham expected to step into Number 10 next week, devolution has shot up the political agenda.
insights.locator: With Andy Burnham expected to step into

insights.claim: English devolution is fundamentally about relocating power and funding from central government to regional institutions, not about redistributing money from rich to poor regions or relocating civil servants out of London.
insights.speaker: Suki Masana
insights.so_what: Framing devolution as a power question rather than a redistribution one sets expectations for what reforms will and won't touch fiscal transfers.
insights.quote: The devolution agenda is ultimately about where power sits.
insights.locator: For those only newly attuned to devolution

insights.claim: The UK is one of the world's most centralised nations, with over 90% of tax revenue collected by central government.
insights.speaker: Suki Masana
insights.so_what: The extreme centralisation baseline means even incremental fiscal devolution is a large structural shift, and there is significant runway for reform.
insights.quote: with over 90% of tax revenue collected by the central government
insights.locator: As the UK is one of the most centralised

insights.claim: The UK's current devolution agenda has run for almost three decades across Blair, coalition, and Conservative governments, producing Devolved Administrations in Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland plus English city-region Mayoral Authorities.
insights.speaker: Suki Masana
insights.so_what: Deep institutional precedent means the incoming government builds on existing structures rather than starting fresh, favouring continuity over radical change.
insights.locator: The current iteration of the UK’s devolution agenda

insights.claim: Oxford Economics expects the gradualist approach to devolution to persist despite the rhetoric, with Mayoral Authorities spreading across the map and gaining powers only as institutional capacity matures.
insights.speaker: Suki Masana
insights.so_what: Investors expecting a rapid decentralisation shock should temper expectations; policy change will be incremental and concentrated in already-mature authorities.
insights.quote: Despite the rhetoric, we don’t think this gradualist approach will change.
insights.locator: Despite the rhetoric, we don’t think

insights.claim: Mature authorities such as the West Midlands and Greater Manchester are expected to see widened powers, growing budget accountability, and some devolution of revenue-retention powers.
insights.speaker: Suki Masana
insights.so_what: The most established city-regions are the near-term winners of expanded fiscal autonomy, concentrating opportunity in a handful of places.
insights.locator: Where there are already mature and capable

insights.claim: The Integrated Settlement, which consolidates central government funds and grants into a single flexible budget for Mayoral Authorities, is the recent major devolution milestone.
insights.speaker: Suki Masana
insights.so_what: Single-budget flexibility and longer-term certainty change how regional funds are deployed, relevant for local infrastructure and growth spending.
insights.locator: The recent major milestone has been the development

insights.claim: The Treasury and Whitehall departments have opposed English devolution for decades, fearing loss of spending control and a confusing patchwork of regional policies.
insights.speaker: Suki Masana
insights.so_what: Entrenched Treasury resistance is the key structural brake on the pace of fiscal devolution, explaining why gradualism prevails.
insights.quote: There has been substantial opposition to English devolution across Whitehall departments and particularly in the Treasury for decades
insights.locator: Our view of continued gradualism is not

insights.claim: Significant progress on rate-setting or debt-raising powers will not happen in the medium term, having been ruled out by the Chancellor in her March Mais lecture and not currently prioritised by Burnham.
insights.speaker: Suki Masana
insights.so_what: Rules out the boldest fiscal-autonomy scenarios; local authorities will not gain independent taxing or borrowing power soon.
insights.quote: What will not happen in the medium-term is significant progress on rate-setting or debt-raising powers.
insights.locator: What will not happen in the medium-term

insights.claim: That mayors do not yet control a visitor levy on overnight stays illustrates how limited English local fiscal power still is.
insights.speaker: Suki Masana
insights.so_what: The low starting base for local revenue tools signals a long, slow road for meaningful fiscal devolution.
insights.quote: that powers over a visitor levy are not already in the hands of mayors shows how far England still has to go
insights.locator: Increased fiscal devolution, particularly in the form

themes.theme: Revenue retention design and regional inequality risk

insights.claim: The biggest step forward will be incremental revenue retention, where mature Mayoral Authorities keep a share of revenues exceeding an agreed baseline to incentivise growth-enhancing local policy.
insights.speaker: Suki Masana
insights.so_what: Revenue retention ties local fiscal upside to growth outcomes, a mechanism that rewards regions able to expand their tax base.
insights.quote: It is designed to raise the incentives for local leaders to pursue economic growth-enhancing policies as they can reap the benefits.
insights.locator: The biggest step forward, and challenge

insights.claim: Revenue retention risks embedding regional inequality because Oxford Economics forecasts the fastest-growing Mayoral Authorities are the wealthiest, like London, while the slowest are low-productivity regions such as West Midlands and South Yorkshire.
insights.speaker: Suki Masana
insights.so_what: A growth-linked retention design could widen the gap between prosperous and lagging UK regions, a key second-order risk for regional investment allocation.
insights.quote: weaker regions could fall into a self-fulfilling cycle of weak growth, limited scope for investment, drained revenues, and yet weaker growth
insights.locator: Most challenging, however, is how do you prevent

insights.claim: A retention settlement available only to the handful of established authorities, while most of England stays on the old system, risks reinforcing a cycle where the most capable places pull further ahead.
insights.speaker: Suki Masana
insights.so_what: Uneven coverage compounds the inequality risk, though it may resolve as more authorities complete reorganisation and qualify over time.
insights.locator: The third point, the coverage question

insights.claim: The central unresolved design tension is how to preserve the growth incentive that makes revenue retention attractive while limiting the regional inequality it risks entrenching.
insights.speaker: Suki Masana
insights.so_what: This trade-off is the defining policy dilemma to watch; how baselines and retention percentages are set will determine winners and losers across UK regions.
insights.quote: how to maintain the growth incentive that makes revenue retention attractive while limiting the regional inequality it risks entrenching
